common_name: Orca Bean (Killer Whale Bean, Half-and-Half Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Orca is a bush dry bean type that matures in 75–90 days to full dry-shell stage; the beans are famous for their striking two-tone coloring — exactly half black, half white on each bean — that resembles the coloring of orca (killer) whales and makes them one of the most visually distinctive dry beans available.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in full sun; harvest for dry beans when pods are fully dry and papery on the vine; in humid climates pull whole plants before complete drying and hang in a sheltered, airy space to finish drying to prevent mold on pods. Orca beans are a relatively modern open-pollinated dry bean variety that quickly gained wide popularity among seed savers and heirloom gardeners for their extraordinary visual appearance: each bean is precisely two-toned, with exactly one half black and one half white in a pattern that closely resembles the distinctive black-and-white coloring of the orca whale — hence the common names 'Orca Bean' and 'Killer Whale Bean.' The coloring is remarkably consistent across all seeds from a plant, though the exact position of the color boundary and the ratio of black to white can vary slightly between individual seeds. The seeds are medium-sized, slightly plump, and oval. When cooked, the dramatic two-tone coloring fades and the beans become a more uniform dark brown to black, as with most vividly colored beans — the show is in the seed, not the cooked dish. The flavor is mild and creamy, similar to black beans, with a pleasantly earthy character. Orca beans are excellent in soups, stews, rice dishes, and any preparation where black beans are used. They are often added to seed displays and mixed bean soups specifically for visual interest, and the raw seeds make a striking addition to craft projects, seed collages, and botanical displays.

common_name: Hutterite Soup Bean (Yellow Hutterite Bean, Great Plains Heritage Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Hutterite is a bush dry bean with buff-yellow seeds from the Hutterite communities of the northern Great Plains — a cold-adapted heritage variety maintained for generations by Hutterite colonies in Montana, South Dakota, and Canada, renowned for its extremely creamy, thick texture when cooked into soups and stews — its primary traditional use as the 'Sunday soup bean' of Hutterite communal kitchens.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in full sun; Hutterite beans tolerate short cool growing seasons (80–95 days makes them practical for zones 3–4 short summers); harvest dry pods when fully papery and rattle when shaken. Hutterite Soup Bean is an heirloom dry bean variety maintained by the Hutterite communities of the northern Great Plains — communal Anabaptist communities of German and Austrian origin who settled the Montana, South Dakota, and Alberta regions in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, bringing their traditional agricultural varieties with them from Europe. The Hutterites maintain strict communal farming traditions including seed saving, and have preserved many heritage varieties of beans, grains, and vegetables that might otherwise have been lost. Hutterite beans have buff-yellow to light cream seeds with a mild, delicate flavor and an unusually thick, creamy texture when cooked — thicker than navy beans or great northern beans, with less tendency to break down or become mushy when simmered long. This creamy texture makes them particularly well-suited for thick soups and stews in the German-Russian culinary tradition of the Hutterite communities — served weekly in communal dining halls as 'bean soup Sunday.' The variety is adapted to the short, cool growing seasons of the northern plains, making it also excellent for short-season northern gardeners who struggle with longer-season dry beans. Good flavor for use in any white-bean recipe; outstanding in soups.

common_name: Flor de Mayo Bean (Mexican May Flower Bean, Flor de Mayo Brillante)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moderately fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Flor de Mayo is one of the most important dry bean varieties in Mexican cuisine — known for its distinctive speckled appearance (magenta, pink, and cream-colored fresh seeds), quick cooking, and mild, sweet flavor that makes it the preferred bean in many regions of central Mexico for everyday frijoles, refried beans, and rice-and-bean dishes.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in full sun; Flor de Mayo is well-adapted to semi-dry, warm conditions and performs particularly well in the hot, dry growing season of zone 7–10 areas with low humidity; harvest when pods are fully dry and papery. Flor de Mayo (Spanish for 'May Flower') is one of the oldest and most culturally significant dry bean varieties in Mexico, where it has been grown for centuries as a primary food staple — ranking among the most commercially important beans in the Mexican market alongside Bayo, Peruano/Canario, and Azufrado varieties. The fresh seeds are distinctively beautiful: oval, slightly plump, and marked with magenta, rose-pink, and cream-white in a speckled and streaked pattern reminiscent of the flowers it was named for; the pattern and color intensify at the fresh-shell stage and fade to a more subdued cream-and-tan speckled pattern when fully dried. When cooked, the beans become uniformly pale tan-brown, but the texture and flavor are the key appeal: Flor de Mayo cooks to a mild, slightly sweet, creamy texture with thin skins that are almost imperceptible — making them one of the quickest-cooking dry beans and one of the smoothest for refried beans where texture matters. In Mexican home cooking, Flor de Mayo is the bean that many home cooks from Jalisco, Nayarit, and Michoacán states grew up eating; it is available in Mexican markets under this name and under regional names including 'Flor de Mayo Brillante' and 'Crema'.
sources: CIMMYT (International Maize and Wheat Improvement Center)

common_name: Arikara Yellow Bean (Ree Bean, Northern Plains Native American Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in full sun; Arikara Yellow is an heirloom bush bean from the Arikara people of the Missouri River basin in present-day North Dakota, adapted to the short growing season and continental extremes of the northern Great Plains — exceptionally quick to mature for a dry bean (80–95 days) and tolerant of cool temperatures at both ends of the season.

directions: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in full sun; Arikara Yellow is drought-tolerant once established and performs well in poor to moderately fertile soils without supplemental irrigation in all but the driest conditions; harvest dry pods before fall rains can induce mold on the dried pods. Arikara Yellow Bean is a traditional heirloom dry bean of the Arikara people (also called the Ree), a Caddoan-speaking people who lived in permanent earth-lodge villages along the Missouri River in present-day North Dakota and South Dakota, where they practiced intensive agriculture including cultivation of corn, squash, sunflowers, and beans for centuries before European contact. The Arikara were important traders in the Missouri River trade network, and their agricultural products — including seeds — were traded widely, distributing their bean varieties to neighboring peoples and eventually to European and American settlers and traders. Arikara Yellow is characterized by small to medium-sized, oval, pale yellow to light tan seeds with a mild, earthy, somewhat buttery flavor that is excellent in soups, stews, and as a baked bean. The variety's most distinctive horticultural characteristic is its extremely short maturity for a dry bean — 80–95 days — which allows it to be grown and harvested in the short frost-free season of zones 3–4 where most dry beans cannot ripen before fall frost. This same short-season adaptation makes Arikara Yellow valuable for gardeners in any region who want a reliable dry bean with insurance against early fall frosts or a compressed summer schedule.

common_name: Red Ripper Southern Pea (Red Ripper Cowpea, Old Timer Pea)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 65°F; Red Ripper is an heirloom Southern pea variety with distinctive reddish-tan to red-brown seeds with darker mottling — one of the most heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant of all Southern pea types, widely used as both a summer vegetable and a warm-season cover crop for building organic matter in hot, dry conditions where other cover crops struggle.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after soil warms; Red Ripper is semi-vining and may need minimal support or can be left to sprawl; for cover crop use broadcast at 5–10 lbs per 1000 sq ft; harvest pods at green-shell stage for fresh eating or allow to fully dry for storage as dry peas. Red Ripper is one of the most heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant Southern pea varieties available, valued for both its role as a food crop and its exceptional performance as a summer cover crop in the hot, dry conditions of the Southern Plains and Gulf Coast. The plants are semi-vining (not quite as bushy as modern varieties, not as long-vining as pole types) and produce prolifically under conditions that would reduce the yield of more refined modern varieties — poor, sandy soil, minimal rain, and sustained heat above 95°F. The seeds are distinctive: larger than many Southern peas, reddish-tan to brown with darker mottling, with a hearty, earthy cowpea flavor when cooked. As a cover crop, Red Ripper builds nitrogen at high rates (80–120 lbs N/acre under good conditions), suppresses summer weeds aggressively with its spreading canopy, and its large biomass decomposes quickly in the warm soil to build organic matter. Texas A&M AgriLife Extension and LSU AgCenter recommend Red Ripper as one of the top summer cover crops for the Gulf South. For food production, Red Ripper is an old heirloom with deep roots in Southern and Texan food traditions — used as fresh shelled peas, dried peas, and as the source of pot likker in traditional Southern cooking.
